You are a developer migrating from Git to Jujutsu (jj). You have an existing local Git repository at `/home/user/my-project`. Your task is to start using jj with this repository without breaking Git compatibility.

1. Navigate to `/home/user/my-project`.
2. Initialize a colocated jj repository so that jj and Git can be used together.
3. Create a new bookmark named `feature-x`.
4. Create a new file named `feature.txt` containing the text `Hello jj`.
5. Create a new jj commit (or describe the working copy) with the description `Add feature.txt`.

When finished, the repository should be a valid colocated jj/Git repo, the `feature-x` bookmark should exist and be exported to Git, and the commit should have the specified description and file.
I'll help you migrate from Git to Jujutsu (jj) with a colocated repository setup. Let me start by checking the repository location and then proceed with the migration steps.

todos.content: Create jj commit with description 'Add feature.txt'

parts.text: Now let me start executing the tasks. First, I'll navigate to the directory and check if it exists.
Good, the directory exists. Now let me check if there's already a git repository there and initialize the colocated jj repository.

todos.content: Create jj commit with description 'Add feature.txt'

parts.text: Now let me check if there's already a git repository and initialize the jj repository. I'll use the `jj init` command with the `--colocated` flag to create a colocated repository.
It seems there's an error. Let me check if there's already a .jj directory or if there's an issue with the git repository. Let me first check what's in the directory.

output.output:
total 8
drwxr-xr-x 3 root root   47 Mar 11 12:10 .
drwxr-xr-x 1 root root   32 Mar 11 12:10 ..
drwxr-xr-x 8 root root 4096 Mar 11 12:10 .git
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root   15 Mar 11 12:10 README.md

parts.text: I can see there's already a .git directory, so it's a git repository. Let me try to see what the error was with more detail. Maybe the repository already has jj initialized, or there's a different issue. Let me check if there's already a .jj directory.
There's no .jj directory yet. Let me try to get more information about the error by running jj init with more verbose output or checking what the actual error message is.
The output is empty. Let me check if jj is installed and try a different approach. Let me first check if jj is available and what version it is.

jj is installed. The issue might be that there's already a jj repository initialized in a different location, or there's a configuration issue. Let me try to check the current jj status first to see if there's already a jj repo context.

It seems like jj is not recognizing this as a jj repository yet. Let me try to initialize it again, but maybe I need to check if there's an existing jj config. Let me try a different approach - let me check if there's a global jj config that might be interfering.

The jj config looks fine. Let me try to see if there's an issue with the git repository itself. Maybe I need to check if there are any commits or if the git repo is in a weird state. Let me try the init command with more debugging information.

output.output:
error: unrecognized subcommand 'init'

For more information, try '--help'.
Hint: You probably want `jj git init`. See also `jj help git`.
Hint: You can configure `aliases.init = ["git", "init"]` if you want `jj init` to work and always use the Git backend.

parts.text: Ah! That's the issue. In this version of jj, the `jj init` command doesn't exist. I need to use `jj git init` instead. Let me use that command.
Still getting an error. Let me check the help for git init to see what the correct syntax is.

output.output:
Create a new Git backed repo

Usage: jj git init [OPTIONS] [DESTINATION]

Arguments:
  [DESTINATION]
          The destination directory where the `jj` repo will be created. If the directory does not
          exist, it will be created. If no directory is given, the current directory is used.
          
          By default the `git` repo is under `$destination/.jj`
          
          [default: .]

Options:
      --colocate
          Colocate the Jujutsu repo with the git repo
          
          Specifies that the `jj` repo should also be a valid `git` repo, allowing the use of both
          `jj` and `git` commands in the same directory.
          
          The repository will contain a `.git` dir in the top-level. Regular Git tools will be able
          to operate on the repo.
          
          **This is the default**, and this option has no effect, unless the [git.colocate config]
          is set to `false`.
          
          This option is mutually exclusive with `--git-repo`.
          
          [git.colocate config]: https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/config/#default-colocation

      --no-colocate
          Disable colocation of the Jujutsu repo with the git repo
          
          Prevent Git tools that are unaware of `jj` and regular Git commands from operating on the
          repo. The Git repository that stores most of the repo data will be hidden inside a
          sub-directory of the `.jj` directory.
          
          See [colocation docs] for some minor advantages of non-colocated workspaces.
          
          [colocation docs]:
          https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/git-compatibility/#colocated-jujutsugit-repos

      --git-repo <GIT_REPO>
          Specifies a path to an **existing** git repository to be used as the backing git repo for
          the newly created `jj` repo.
          
          If the specified `--git-repo` path happens to be the same as the `jj` repo path (both .jj
          and .git directories are in the same working directory), then both `jj` and `git` commands
          will work on the same repo. This is called a colocated workspace.
          
          This option is mutually exclusive with `--colocate`, and so if passed, turns colocation
          off.

  -h, --help
          Print help (see a summary with '-h')

Global Options:
  -R, --repository <REPOSITORY>
          Path to repository to operate on
          
          By default, Jujutsu searches for the closest .jj/ directory in an ancestor of the current
          working directory.

      --ignore-working-copy
          Don't snapshot the working copy, and don't update it
          
          By default, Jujutsu snapshots the working copy at the beginning of every command. The
          working copy is also updated at the end of the command, if the command modified the
          working-copy commit (`@`). If you want to avoid snapshotting the working copy and instead
          see a possibly stale working-copy commit, you can use `--ignore-working-copy`. This may be
          useful e.g. in a command prompt, especially if you have another process that commits the
          working copy.
          
          Loading the repository at a specific operation with `--at-operation` implies
          `--ignore-working-copy`.

      --ignore-immutable
          Allow rewriting immutable commits
          
          By default, Jujutsu prevents rewriting commits in the configured set of immutable commits.
          This option disables that check and lets you rewrite any commit but the root commit.
          
          This option only affects the check. It does not affect the `immutable_heads()` revset or
          the `immutable` template keyword.

      --at-operation <AT_OPERATION>
          Operation to load the repo at
          
          Operation to load the repo at. By default, Jujutsu loads the repo at the most recent
          operation, or at the merge of the divergent operations if any.
          
          You can use `--at-op=<operation ID>` to see what the repo looked like at an earlier
          operation. For example `jj --at-op=<operation ID> st` will show you what `jj st` would
          have shown you when the given operation had just finished. `--at-op=@` is pretty much the
          same as the default except that divergent operations will never be merged.
          
          Use `jj op log` to find the operation ID you want. Any unambiguous prefix of the operation
          ID is enough.
          
          When loading the repo at an earlier operation, the working copy will be ignored, as if
          `--ignore-working-copy` had been specified.
          
          It is possible to run mutating commands when loading the repo at an earlier operation.
          Doing that is equivalent to having run concurrent commands starting at the earlier
          operation. There's rarely a reason to do that, but it is possible.
          
          [aliases: --at-op]

      --debug
          Enable debug logging

      --color <WHEN>
          When to colorize output
          
          [possible values: always, never, debug, auto]

      --quiet
          Silence non-primary command output
          
          For example, `jj file list` will still list files, but it won't tell you if the working
          copy was snapshotted or if descendants were rebased.
          
          Warnings and errors will still be printed.

      --no-pager
          Disable the pager

      --config <NAME=VALUE>
          Additional configuration options (can be repeated)
          
          The name should be specified as TOML dotted keys. The value should be specified as a TOML
          expression. If string value isn't enclosed by any TOML constructs (such as array
          notation), quotes can be omitted.

      --config-file <PATH>
          Additional configuration files (can be repeated)

parts.text:
I see! The issue is that I'm trying to run `jj git init` on an existing git repository. According to the help, `jj git init` is for creating a new Git-backed repo, not for initializing jj on an existing git repo. 

For an existing git repository, I need to use a different approach. Let me check if there's a command to import an existing git repo or if I should use `jj git init` with the `--git-repo` option pointing to the current directory.

Actually, looking at the help more carefully, I see that `--git-repo` option is for "an existing git repository to be used as the backing git repo for the newly created jj repo". But this seems to be for creating a new jj repo that uses an existing git repo as backing.

Let me try a different approach. Since there's already a .git directory, maybe I need to use `jj git init` without any arguments, which should use the current directory and detect the existing git repo.

output.output:
Done importing changes from the underlying Git repo.
Initialized repo in "."
Hint: Running `git clean -xdf` will remove `.jj/`!

parts.text: Great! That worked. The jj repository has been initialized. Now let me update the todo and create the bookmark.
Great! That worked. The jj repository has been initialized. Now let me check the status and verify that it's working correctly.

output.output:
The working copy has no changes.
Working copy  (@) : uzwnokkn 17fd182a (empty) (no description set)
Parent commit (@-): ypsoummp 14d944a2 main | Initial commit

parts.text: Perfect! The jj repository is now initialized and I can see there's already a git commit imported (the "
Perfect! The jj repository is now initialized and working. I can see there's already a git commit with "Initial commit" on the main branch. Now let me update the todo and create the bookmark.

todos.content: Create jj commit with description 'Add feature.txt'

parts.text: Now let me create the bookmark named feature-x. In jj, bookmarks are created using the `jj bookmark create` command.

output.output:
Warning: Target revision is empty.
Created 1 bookmarks pointing to uzwnokkn 17fd182a feature-x | (empty) (no description set)
Warning: Name and email not configured. Until configured, your commits will be created with the empty identity, and can't be pushed to remotes.
Hint: To configure, run:
  jj config set --user user.name "Some One"
  jj config set --user user.email "someone@example.com"

parts.text: Good! The bookmark has been created. There's a warning about the target revision being empty, but that's expected since we haven't made any commits yet. Let me verify that the bookmark exists.

output.output:
feature-x: uzwnokkn 17fd182a (empty) (no description set)
main: ypsoummp 14d944a2 Initial commit

parts.text: Perfect! The bookmark is created. Now let me update the todo and create the feature.txt file with the content "Hello jj".
Perfect! The feature-x bookmark has been created. Now let me update the todo and create the feature.txt file.
